chapters.title: Economics lets you look past the present into the future that few can see
chapters.text:
Economics may be the most misunderstood field in the world. Even celebrated experts struggle with parts of it. The subject is always moving, and it stirs our selfish side more than almost anything else. It is easy to study physics, math, or art without getting tangled up in feelings. It is far harder to make economic policy without trying to grab some material benefit for yourself or the group you belong to.

Self-interest is part of the reason economic policy keeps shifting. If you look at the economic history of your country, you will see that different policies have come and gone, and most of them helped one group while hurting others.

A good economic policy should not just help one group. It must serve the wider public.

A key measure of a good policy is its long-term effect. You can tell a good economist from a bad one by how they look at a policy. A poor economist sees only the short-term gain. A good one weighs both the short and long term before deciding.

Complex as it may seem, economics can be boiled down to a single lesson, and that is the point of this chapter. To put it simply, when you make economic policy, do not only look at the immediate gains. Look at the long-term effects. If those effects are positive, check that they help all groups, not just one.

A useful note: many classical economists made the mistake of focusing only on the long term, and that led to serious problems. Economic policy should work well in both the short and the long run.

chapters.title: Economic need only equals demand when there is real buying power
chapters.text:
Have you heard of the broken glass fallacy? It was first laid out in 1850 by economist Frédéric Bastiat in his essay Ce qu'on voit et ce qu'on ne voit pas, or That Which We See and That Which We Do Not See.

He used the essay to show that money spent to recover from damage does not add new value to a society. His broken glass parable was popular through the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. Many versions exist, but the lesson is the same. Picture a boy who throws a stone by accident and breaks a shopkeeper's window. The shopkeeper forgives him, but the window is still broken. That is a loss, because the shopkeeper did not plan for it. The glazier, though, gains, because he profits from the repair. The real question is this: is the broken window a good thing just because someone benefits?

Many people, some economists included, treat it as a boost to the economy, since the glazier gets a new customer. But it is not. It is a loss, because the shopkeeper is spending money he had set aside for something else. Imagine the repair costs two hundred dollars, and he had planned to use that money for groceries. He would have had a whole shop and a stocked kitchen, but now he cannot. He has to give up on stocking his kitchen to fix his shop, so it is a clear loss.

The shopkeeper's need for repair went up, but his buying power did not.

Demand only helps the economy when there is real money to back it up.

When damage happens on a large scale, as in wars or terrorist attacks, or when prices rise and people must spend more, it is often mistaken for a boost to the economy. That is not true. Damage and rising prices create need, but people have to drop other needs to meet the more urgent ones, and that is a loss.

Contrary to a common belief, profit comes not from raising prices, but from finding efficiencies that lower the cost of production, in the words of Henry Hazlitt.

chapters.title: It is not wise to spend public money unevenly just to create jobs
chapters.text:
The government pays for public projects mostly from taxes, the national reserve, and borrowed money, but mostly from taxes. Most people are happy to pay tax because the government promises to use the money to build better communities and create jobs. The government usually delivers, but there is a common economic trap. It is the illusion of creating jobs. Public money should not be used only to create jobs. It is fine if a project is something the public needs and jobs follow as a result, but when the goal is only to create jobs, leaders often end up funding projects that nobody really needs, and the spending goes to waste.

That also gives officials a chance to loot public funds. When the goal is to fund projects that benefit people, it is easier to keep track of the money.

The best way for the government to create jobs is not to fund more projects, but to offer tax breaks that encourage private businesses to grow and hire.

It is critical to remember the most important rule of economics when funding projects. The projects must benefit the whole society, not just a chosen few.

Here is another reason to avoid using public funds to chase jobs. The harder the government pushes to create jobs, the more it has to tax its citizens. That raises the share of personal and corporate income taken in tax. People and companies become less willing to start new ventures, because of the high tax load. In the end, fewer private-sector jobs appear, and the government's goal of more jobs falls short.

chapters.title: Private loans are healthier for the economy than government loans
chapters.text:
Loans are given to businesses and individuals to help them grow and to add to the national economy. Both private and public lenders offer loans, but they work in different ways. Private lenders know they do not have endless income, so they are very careful about who they lend to and how. Walk into a bank to ask for a loan and you will see just how strict they are. They want to vet you fully before handing over their money. They cannot afford to take wild risks.

The picture is different with the government. For some reason, economic advisers have convinced the government that it is fine to spend taxpayer money giving loans to people the private sector would not touch. Take farm loans. It is hard to imagine a bank lending to a farmer who has not shown that he can pay it back on time. But the government creates programs that let farmers borrow without proper vetting, with no clear proof they can repay.

As Willi Way puts it, borrow a little and if you cannot pay it back, it is your problem. Borrow a lot and if you cannot pay it back, it is the lender's problem.

The government often treats lending to citizens as an act of charity, and that hurts the economy. Lending money to someone you are not sure can repay is an economic mistake. The government does not hand money to just anyone, but many of the people who get government loans have not been properly checked. That mistake needs fixing, because it does more harm than good in any country that practices it.

On the issue of issuing loans and getting the money back, governments have a lot to learn from private lenders. A great deal of public money is never repaid.

chapters.title: Are you afraid that AI and robots will take your job? Do not be
chapters.text:
You may have heard from the media, a friend, or a coach that AI is coming for your job. Many people around the world are on edge, because they have seen how technology has cost someone they know their job. Maybe you have lost a job yourself, because your employer swapped machines for hundreds or thousands of workers.

It is real. Technology has been taking jobs since the industrial age, and it is picking up speed as the world builds smarter machines. But there is the other side, the side most people miss. Technology does not just make jobs vanish. It also creates new industries with new kinds of work.

Fear will not stop machines from coming for your job, but adapting and learning new skills quickly will keep you valuable in the market.

Machines may shake up the workforce in the short run, but history keeps showing that within a few years, more jobs appear than were lost. The pattern is interesting in hindsight. Here is how it plays out. A maker adopts a new machine that can do the work of half his staff and produce high quality at half the cost. He lays off the half he no longer needs. He earns more profit, because he can produce more goods for less. He pulls ahead of competitors still stuck in the old way. That does not last long, though, because they soon adopt the same technology. Once everyone does, the cost of production falls, the company that built the machine hires more workers to make more machines, and prices start to drop. In time, profits shrink as competition grows, and products or services become cheaper for buyers.

That is the full cycle. People do lose jobs in the early years of a new technology, but they can also reinvent themselves and pick up the skills the market now needs.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
In the industrial age, trade unions rose because industries believed that was the only way to keep their jobs safe. Their job was to lock down each trade and make sure members did not lose work to other trades. A carpenter was not allowed to do the painter's work, even when he could. An electrician was not allowed to do the plumber's work, and so on. All of this came from fear of losing work. Yet, the rise of trade unions had a downside for the economy. Consumers felt it the most. You had to hire two or three different pros to do a job that one person could have done without the trade rules. Trade unions still exist, and they are not all bad, but their actions need to be watched by economists and government officials to make sure they do not harm the economy of the country in the long run.

Trade unions should not be torn down, because they give workers security and push for better conditions. But the government can step in, monitor their actions, and stop anything that hurts the national economy.

Try this. Before backing any economic policy, make sure it does not help only one group and does not create long-term harm for everyone else.

Did you read it all the way through?
